mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} and \mathcal {D} be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from \mathcal {C} to \mathcal {D} is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each A,B: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, a function \mathbf {hom} _{ F } : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbf {ob} _{ F } A , \mathbf {ob} _{ F } B \right ) \mathclose {}}.

mainmatter.p: In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs.

Let \mathcal {C} and \mathcal {D} be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from \mathcal {C} to \mathcal {D} is given by a wild 0-functor F : \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} equipped with, for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( C , D \right ) \mathclose {}} and g : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( C , D \right ) \mathclose {}}, an identification \mathsf { seq } _F \, f \, g : \mathbf {hom} _{ F } { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} = \mathbf {hom} _{ F } {f}; \mathbf {hom} _{ G } {g} .
Let \mathcal {C} be a wild category; the identity 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ 1 _{ \mathcal {C} } }}{ \mathcal {C} } on \mathcal {C} is defined as follows:
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:

Let \mathcal {C} and \mathcal {D} be wild categories, and let D: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } be an object of \mathcal {D}. We define the constant 0-functor on D: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } out of \mathcal {C} to be the 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ \Delta D }}{ \mathcal {D} } defined as follows:
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:
A wild category is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each A,B: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, a family of types \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}};
for each A: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, an element 1 _{ A } : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , A \right ) \mathclose {}};

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}}, an element { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , C \right ) \mathclose {}}

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}, a identification \mathsf { lunit } _{ \mathcal {C} } f : { \mathopen {} \left ( 1 _{ A } ;f \right ) \mathclose {}} = f and a identification \mathsf { runit } _{ \mathcal {C} } f : { \mathopen {} \left ( f; 1 _{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}} =f;

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}} and g: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( B , C \right ) \mathclose {}} and h: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( C , D \right ) \mathclose {}}, a identification \mathsf { assoc } _{ \mathcal {C} } \, f \, g \, h : f; { \mathopen {} \left ( g;h \right ) \mathclose {}} = { \mathopen {} \left ( f;g \right ) \mathclose {}} ;h.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} and \mathcal {D} be wild categories, and let D: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } be an object of \mathcal {D}. We define the constant 0-functor on D: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {D} } out of \mathcal {C} to be the 0-functor { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ \Delta D }}{ \mathcal {D} } defined as follows:
The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:

Let \mathcal {C} , \mathcal {D} be two wild categories, and let F,G: \mathcal {C} \to \mathcal {D} be two wild 0-functors from \mathcal {C} to \mathcal {D}. A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each A: \mathbf {ob} _{ \mathcal {C} }, an element \alpha _{A} : \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {D} } { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbf {ob} _{ F } A , \mathbf {ob} _{ G } A \right ) \mathclose {}};

for each f: \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathcal {C} } { \mathopen {} \left ( A , B \right ) \mathclose {}}, an identification \alpha _{f} : \alpha _A; \mathbf {hom} _{ G } {f} = \mathbf {hom} _{ F } {f}; \alpha _B.
